banner: Week 31 · Similar Triangles & the AA Similarity Postulate
essential_question: If two triangles share just two pairs of congruent angles, do you already know — before measuring a single side — that every pair of corresponding sides shares the same ratio?
objective: Define similar triangles as having congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides, apply the AA Similarity Postulate to justify that two triangles are similar, and use a proportion to compute a scale factor or solve for a missing side.

content.prompt: Picture a photo of a triangle, and a poster made by enlarging that exact photo. Before you learn a single new word: what stays exactly the same between the small photo and the big poster, and what changes? Say it out loud in terms of angles and sides.

agenda.title: I do — Same Angles, Proportional Sides

content.heading: Similar triangles: two promises, one postulate
bullets: Similar figures have the same SHAPE but not necessarily the same SIZE — corresponding angles congruent, corresponding sides proportional (all in the same ratio).
Notation: △ABC ~ △DEF. Order matters exactly like congruence notation — A↔D, B↔E, C↔F.
AA (Angle-Angle) Similarity Postulate: if two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another, the triangles are similar — no side ever needs checking.
Why two angles are enough: a triangle's angles always sum to 180°, so once two pairs match, the third pair is FORCED to match too — the same leftover, both times.
Scale factor = the constant ratio between corresponding sides. 'Corresponding parts of similar figures are proportional' — write a proportion and cross-multiply to find a missing side.

content.heading: Same angles hold. Watch the ratio hold too.
content.note: On the sim, drag the scale-factor slider. △DEF is always a scaled copy of △ABC. Watch all three angle measures stay identical between the two triangles while every side pair's ratio locks to the same number as the slider — that number IS the scale factor. Try the 'New Triangle' button to reshape △ABC and confirm the same thing happens no matter the shape.

agenda.title: You do — Scale It, Solve It

content.preview: Build session: measure the height of a tall object you can't reach, using nothing but a shadow (or a mirror) and similar triangles.

success_criteria.text: I can state the definition of similar triangles — congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides
I can apply the AA Similarity Postulate to justify that two triangles are similar from just two angle pairs
I can compute a scale factor and use a proportion to solve for a missing side in similar triangles

questions.prompt: Two triangles have two pairs of congruent angles. By the AA Similarity Postulate, the triangles are…
options: neither — a third angle must be checked first
neither — at least one side must be checked first
questions.explain: AA needs only two angle pairs; the third pair is automatically forced to match by the 180° angle sum, so no side or third angle ever needs checking.

questions.prompt: In similar (but not congruent) triangles, corresponding sides are always…
questions.explain: Similarity relaxes congruence's 'equal sides' promise to 'sides in one constant ratio' — the scale factor.

questions.prompt: Triangle 1 has angles measuring 35° and 95°. Triangle 2 has angles measuring 95° and 50°. Are the two triangles similar? Use AA to justify your answer, including what the third angle of EACH triangle must be.
questions.answer: Yes, similar. Triangle 1's third angle is 180° − 35° − 95° = 50°, giving angles {35°, 95°, 50°}. Triangle 2's third angle is 180° − 95° − 50° = 35°, giving angles {95°, 50°, 35°} — the same three angles. Two pairs match directly (95° and 50°), so AA applies.
questions.explain: Once the third angle of each triangle is computed, both triangles turn out to share the exact same three angle measures — confirming AA similarity even though it wasn't obvious from the two angles given at first glance.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw two similar (but NOT congruent) triangles. Mark two pairs of corresponding angles with matching arc marks to justify the similarity by AA.
questions.answer: Two triangles, clearly different sizes but the same shape, with two pairs of angles arc-marked alike (same style per corresponding pair).
questions.explain: Rubric: two same-shape, different-size triangles drawn (1 pt) + two pairs of corresponding angles correctly and consistently arc-marked (1 pt).

questions.prompt: △ABC ~ △DEF. AB = 6 and DE = 15. What is the scale factor from △ABC to △DEF? If BC = 8, what is EF?
questions.explain: The scale factor is the ratio of any pair of corresponding sides (DE ÷ AB); once found, multiply every other side of △ABC by that same factor to get its match in △DEF.

questions.prompt: Two similar triangles share the same two base angles. The smaller triangle has sides 5, 7, and 8 opposite its three angles. In the larger, similar triangle, the side corresponding to the 5 measures 10. Find X, the side corresponding to the 8.
questions.explain: Corresponding parts of similar figures are proportional — once one pair of corresponding sides gives the scale factor, every other corresponding side scales by that same factor.

questions.prompt: Exit: name two real-world similar figures (a photo and an enlargement, a model car and the real car, a small and large print of the same picture) and explain what must be true about their corresponding angles AND corresponding sides for them to actually be similar — not just 'kind of look the same.'
